# Source type: pdf

--- Page 1 ---
Brainstorming & its variations

--- Page 2 ---
Brainstorming?
• Brainstorming is a technique intended to rapidly 
generate a large number of solution ideas to a 
problem
• The method was first popularized in the late 1930s by 
Alex Osborn in a book called “Applied Imagination”

--- Page 3 ---
Structure of a Classical Brainstorming Session
• Ice-breaking (get to know each other)
• Phase I: Idea Purge Phase
– The problem is stated clearly
– Every group member quickly writes his or her ideas on a 
sheet of paper (2 min in total)
– 4~12 members (diversity of thinking)
• Phase II: Idea Trigger Phase
– Leader calls each member for their ideas
– Team members read their ideas
– Leader writes them down on a board
– If some ideas trigger other ideas, members should write 
them down
– Repeat until no more new ideas are generated
– Sort and organize the ideas
• Phase III: Idea Evaluation & Solution Recommendation 
Phase
– In its final stage of a brainstorming session, the group (or a 
different group) evaluate the ideas and select one or a few 
as the solutions to the problem proposed to the group
Creative 
thinking
Judgmental 
thinking

--- Page 4 ---
Two fundamental principles
• Delayed judgment:
– Judgmental thinking vs. creative thinking
– Judgmental thinking inhibits creative thinking
– Delaying judgment is for generating as many ideas as possible
– Delaying judgment eliminates fear (a mental barrier to 
creativity)
• Focus on quantity:
– The “1/300” law (Makoto, 2007)
• “When you generate 1000 ideas, only 3 of them are 
useful.”
– Linus Pauling:
• “One must endeavor to come up with many ideas — then 
discard the useless ones”

--- Page 5 ---
Basic rules for brainstorming
• Basic rules
– No criticism: think first-judge later
– Focus on quantity (even fragmented ideas or keywords are ok)
• “Quantity bears quality”
– Keep short & snappy
– Don’t settle for the first idea (avoid any fixation)
– Support each other (never criticize)
– Accept unusual ideas
– Combine, improve, expand (hitchhiking/piggybacking)
– Relax
– Have fun
– Laugh
– The wilder, the better
– Try to be creative (consciously try to eliminate mental barriers)
– Reduce the social inhibitions that occur in groups, and therefore, 
stimulate the generation of new ideas
– Try to create a dynamic synergy that will increase the creativity of 
the group

--- Page 6 ---
Types of Mental Barriers
• False assumptions and nonexistent limitations
• Rigid or inappropriate solution strategies
• Overcomplicating problems
• Information overload or scarcity
• Miscommunication and misunderstanding
• Irrelevant or distracting associations (e.g., getting misled by false 
analogies)
• Emotional, cultural, and environmental barriers
• Fear
• Difficulty balancing divergent (creative) and convergent (critical) 
thinking
• Falling in love with an idea
• Paralysis from overabundance of resources

--- Page 7 ---
Nine circles problem
• Without lifting your pen, draw no more than 4 straight 
lines to cross all the nine circles

--- Page 8 ---
Some solutions
• Can you come up with other solutions? 

--- Page 9 ---
4 matchsticks problem
• Using 4 identical matchsticks, can you create an image 
of two equilateral triangles? 

--- Page 10 ---
Killer phrases that easily kill a brainstorming session

--- Page 11 ---
Blamestorming

--- Page 12 ---
About the leader
• The leader’s role is to record and write down all the 
ideas
• The leader should not direct or influence the 
brainstorming
• The leader is a facilitator or recorder, not a boss

--- Page 13 ---
Class Activity #1
• Brainstorming exercise

--- Page 14 ---
An “optimization” analogy to brainstorming

--- Page 15 ---
Visualization and prototyping can be helpful
• In many cases, sketching ideas or building quick 
models can be more effective than simply writing 
them down.
• Some expert brainstormers even bring a “play kit” of 
Lego parts, play dough, duct tape, styrofoam, and 
other materials to quickly prototype their ideas during 
the session.

--- Page 16 ---
Play-doh

--- Page 17 ---
Visual brainstorming (brain drawing)
• A variation of brainstorming
• Participants express their ideas visually instead of with 
words

--- Page 18 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing

--- Page 19 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Ice-breaking and evaluation apprehension
– Ice-breaking facilitates information sharing among group 
members
– Reduces evaluation apprehension by creating psychological 
safety
– Evaluation apprehension:
• Reduces idea variety
• Requires mentally demanding simulation of others’ 
reactions
• Tolls working memory (WM), consuming cognitive 
resources

--- Page 20 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Sequential phases and cognitive efficiency
– Sequential structure:
• Idea purge phase → Idea trigger phase → Evaluation and 
recommendation.
– Benefits:
• Reduces mode switching between mental tasks
– Minimizing mode switching supports the same goal as 
reducing the number of active channels during 
selective attention
• Prevents cognitive overload from multitasking (e.g., 
generating + evaluating)
• Helps group focus on one cognitive process at a time

--- Page 21 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Idea purging and long-term memory (LTM)
– Idea purging = Retrieving information from LTM
– Influenced by:
• How the problem is defined
• Perceptual style and individual differences
• Bottom-up and top-down processing
• Internal knowledge networks
– Decision making involved in selecting relevant ideas from 
internal stimuli

--- Page 22 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Writing aids cognition
– Writing down ideas:
– Offloads WM → Frees capacity for manipulation.
– Facilitates embodied cognition:
• Spatial proximity of written ideas → Natural associations

--- Page 23 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Idea triggering and WM
– Triggering relies on WM for mental manipulation
– Selective attention when choosing ideas on shared 
screen/board
– Influences:
• Bottom-up (visual salience)
• Top-down (goals and expectations)
– Fixation = Failure to move attention away from limited cues.
– Focused attention needed to manipulate selected items

--- Page 24 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Production blocking and turn-taking
• Participants must wait to speak:
– Bottlenecks idea flow
– Creates production blocking
• Waiting period:
– Increases risk of forgetting ideas (WM loss)
– Multitasking required: Listen + hold idea in WM

--- Page 25 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Evaluation phase and bias
– Idea evaluation and solution recommendation = Decision 
making phase.
– Prone to bias:
• Social dynamics (dominance, conformity)
• Premature filtering of ideas.

--- Page 26 ---
Brainstorming and human information processing
• Summary
– Group brainstorming involves complex cognitive activities:
• Retrieval from LTM
• WM manipulation
• Decision making
• Attention control
– Cognitive bottlenecks:
• Evaluation apprehension
• Production blocking
• Multitasking demands
– Structuring and writing can help reduce cognitive load and 
improve output

--- Page 27 ---
Strengths of brainstorming
• Generates a large number of ideas in a short time
• Encourages free thinking and creativity
• Builds on each other’s ideas (synergy and idea cross-
pollination)
• Creates a supportive group atmosphere that meets 
social-emotional needs, fosters motivation, and 
strengthens cohesion (if facilitated well)
• Reduces fear of judgment when the “no criticism” rule 
is followed
• Provides diverse perspectives that individuals alone 
may not generate

--- Page 28 ---
Weaknesses of brainstorming
• May be limited to relatively simple or well-defined problems
• Generated ideas can be low-quality or superficial without proper 
facilitation
• May be less efficient than individual work for certain tasks (low return on 
investment of time and effort)
• Risk of group dynamics issues:
– Some members may dominate the discussion
– Others may feel shy, intimidated, or reluctant to contribute
– Conflicts can arise when volatile issues are discussed
• Delaying judgment is difficult in practice, leading to premature evaluation 
of ideas
• Hard to give due credit to individuals for contributions
• Typically generates only one idea at a time, slowing the process
• Susceptible to groupthink, where conformity pressure reduces originality
• Can encourage quantity over quality, with too much focus on idea counts
• Requires skilled facilitation; without it, sessions often lose focus or drift

--- Page 29 ---
Variations of brainstorming

--- Page 30 ---
Individual Brainstorming
• Brainstorming can also be done by a single individual.
• Recent studies suggest that when multiple people 
brainstorm individually and then pool their ideas, the 
results can sometimes be better than those from a 
group brainstorming session.

--- Page 31 ---
Brainwriting (Method 635) 
• A variation of brainstorming designed to overcome its 
weaknesses

--- Page 32 ---
Brainwriting (Method 635) 

--- Page 33 ---
Brainwriting form

--- Page 34 ---
Brainwriting (Pool Method)

--- Page 35 ---
Comparison of group brainstorming, individual 
brainstorming and brainwriting
Method Advantages Disadvantages
Group 
Brainstorming
- Real-time idea exchange 
and dynamic interaction
- Builds on group energy
- Can spark spontaneous 
ideas
- Production blocking: Since only one person can speak at a time, 
others must wait, which can slow down idea generation and lead to 
information loss in working memory as participants suppress or 
forget ideas while waiting
- Evaluation apprehension—the fear of being judged—requires mental 
simulation and decision-making, which is cognitively demanding and 
can hinder idea generation
- Requires multitasking: listening while thinking, which can overload 
the cognitive system due to overlapping cognitive resources
Individual 
Brainstorming
- Time-efficient (no waiting)
- No evaluation 
apprehension
- Full focus on one’s own 
thoughts
- No stimulation from others’ ideas
- No immediate feedback or collaborative idea building
Brainwriting - Everyone writes ideas 
simultaneously (parallel 
process)
- No evaluation 
apprehension
- Encourages structured 
building on others’ ideas
- Promotes equal 
participation
- Less spontaneous and dynamic than spoken brainstorming 
- Can be slower in large groups (ideas take time to circulate) 
- Less verbal interaction

--- Page 36 ---
Sorting and organizing ideas 
(brainstorming & brainwriting)

--- Page 37 ---
Sorting & organizing ideas

• Sticky Notes

--- Page 39 ---
Reverse Brainstorming

--- Page 40 ---

--- Page 41 ---
Reverse Brainstorming

--- Page 42 ---
Reverse Brainstorming

--- Page 43 ---
Reverse Brainstorming Exercise
• Refrigerator design

--- Page 44 ---
Reverse brainstorming
• Reverse brainstorming helps overcome fixation
• Fixation = Getting stuck on a narrow set of ideas due 
to focused attention
• Reversing the problem changes how it is viewed, 
which changes what is retrieved from long-term 
memory
• Ideas generated from the reversed problem are still 
related to the original one
• This approach helps explore new areas of the solution 
space and break free from fixation

--- Page 45 ---
Bodystorming
• Bodystorming is a technique of physically experiencing 
a situation to generate new ideas. It involves setting 
up an experience — complete with necessary artifacts 
and people — and then physically “testing” it.
• It can also include changing your physical space 
during ideation. The focus is on how you interact with 
your environment and the choices you make while 
engaging in it.

--- Page 46 ---
Bodystorming
